relevant_files.role: Core security scanner that orchestrates pattern matching and diff analysis
Regex-based pattern engine for security scanning
Embedded JSON database of security patterns and regex rules
Integration tests for security scanner with fixture-based testing
Documentation for security scanning features and usage

conventions.commits: Conventional Commits with scope, signed-off-by, and GPG signing required
conventions.testing: Unit tests in src, integration tests in tests/, criterion benchmarks, and security-specific tests
conventions.linting: Clippy with deny warnings, rustfmt, and REUSE compliance
conventions.error_handling: thiserror for libraries, anyhow for applications, tracing for logging
conventions.import/module_organization: Crate-based modularization, clear separation of concerns, workspace structure

patterns: Hybrid diff/file scanning with ignore rules
Centralized pattern database in JSON with severity/confidence metadata
SARIF output for GitHub Code Scanning integration
File extension filtering for language-specific patterns

related_issues.title: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection
test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Documents known limitation of current regex-based approach

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Merged PR implementing the test for issue #735

constraints: Current scanner operates line-by-line, cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities where source and sink are on different lines
Adding tree-sitter would require additional dependencies for each language grammar (rust, python, javascript, etc.)
Binary size increase from tree-sitter grammar crates
Tree-sitter queries use Scheme-like syntax with learning curve
Data-flow/taint tracking still requires custom logic on top of AST
test_coverage: Comprehensive test suite including unit tests, integration tests with fixtures, benchmarks, and negative tests documenting known limitations. Security-specific tests cover hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, command injection, XSS, weak crypto, and path traversal. The test_multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected test explicitly documents the regex limitation.

library_findings.relevant_api: Parser creation, language loading, tree parsing, query execution, incremental parsing
library_findings.notes: Tree-sitter provides incremental parsing, language-aware syntax trees, and query capabilities. Rust API is mature and well-documented. Query language is Scheme-like but powerful for pattern matching. Supports WASM for web usage. Binary size impact from grammar crates (tree-sitter-rust ~2MB, tree-sitter-python ~1.5MB, etc.).

library_findings.relevant_api: LANGUAGE constant, query files, syntax tree navigation, field-based queries
library_findings.notes: Rust-specific grammar for parsing Rust code. Provides syntax tree with field information (e.g., function names, variable bindings). Query syntax supports capturing specific nodes and relationships. Integration with tree-sitter core is straightforward via set_language().

approaches.name: Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter Scanner
approaches.description: Maintain current regex-based scanner for simple patterns (hardcoded secrets, weak crypto) while adding tree-sitter-based scanning for multi-line vulnerabilities (SQL injection, command injection, XSS). Implement a new ASTScanner that works alongside SecurityScanner, with language-specific parsers loaded on-demand. Use tree-sitter queries to detect patterns like (call_expression function: (identifier) @sink) with (identifier) @source in scope.
pros: Preserves existing functionality and performance for simple patterns
Adds AST-aware detection for multi-line vulnerabilities
Incremental parsing enables fast re-scans on edits
Maintains backward compatibility with existing pattern database
cons: Increased binary size (~5-10MB depending on grammars)
Language grammar dependencies must be managed
Query syntax learning curve for maintainers
Cold start parsing overhead for large files

approaches.name: Full Tree-Sitter Migration
approaches.description: Replace the entire regex-based PatternEngine with tree-sitter queries. Convert all 14 regex patterns to tree-sitter queries. Implement a new AST-based pattern engine that parses files and executes queries. Maintain same Finding output format for compatibility. Add tree-sitter-rust, tree-sitter-python, tree-sitter-javascript, and tree-sitter-go grammars as dependencies.
pros: Consistent AST-based approach across all patterns
Language-aware parsing eliminates false positives/negatives from regex
Better handling of language syntax variations
Cleaner architecture with unified parsing strategy
cons: Major breaking change requiring extensive testing
Significant refactoring of pattern database (14 patterns to queries)
Larger binary size impact (~10-20MB with multiple grammars)

approaches.name: Tree-Sitter for Multi-Line Only (Spike Approach)
approaches.description: Create a focused spike: implement tree-sitter-rust only for detecting multi-line SQL injection patterns as a proof of concept. Add a new TaintScanner module that tracks data flow from source (user input) to sink (execute/query). Use tree-sitter to parse Rust code and implement taint tracking logic. Keep regex scanner for all other patterns. This validates the approach before full migration.
pros: Validates tree-sitter effectiveness for the core problem
Serves as proof of concept for maintainers
cons: Doesn't solve the full problem (only multi-line SQL injection)
Limited scope may not justify the complexity
Requires additional refactoring if approach is validated
Pattern database remains split between regex and tree-sitter
May not catch all multi-line patterns across languages

recommendation: Implement the Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter Scanner approach. This provides the best balance between solving the core problem (multi-line vulnerability detection) while preserving existing functionality and minimizing risk. Start with a spike implementation for SQL injection in Rust, then expand to other languages and patterns. This approach addresses issue #737's requirements without a full migration, maintaining backward compatibility and allowing incremental adoption. The hybrid model leverages tree-sitter's strengths for complex patterns while keeping regex for simple, high-impact cases.
